# Hospitality Analysis Catalog

Master catalog of high-value analyses for hospitality businesses. Each entry is tagged by segment,
impact type, and typical financial uplift. Use this catalog during gap analysis (Operating Loop
Step 4.2) to identify what a client is **not** measuring but should be.

---

## How to Read This Catalog

| Column | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **#** | Reference ID for cross-linking |
| **Analysis** | Descriptive name |
| **Segment** | H = Hotel/Resort, F = F&B, C = Co-working/Flex, A = All |
| **Impact Type** | `Rev+` = Revenue uplift, `Cost−` = Cost reduction, `Both` |
| **Typical Impact** | Industry-observed range (% of relevant revenue or cost line) |
| **Key Metrics** | Primary KPIs involved |
| **Blind Spot** | What most operators miss without this analysis |

---

## Revenue Generation Analyses

### R01 — Dynamic Pricing / Rate Optimization
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 5–15% ADR uplift when moving from static to algorithmic pricing |
| Key Metrics | ADR, RevPAR, Demand Forecast, Booking Pace, Competitor Rates |
| Blind Spot | Most properties still use seasonal rate cards; they leave money on the table during unexpected demand spikes and fail to stimulate demand in soft periods |

### R02 — Channel Mix Optimization
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Rev+ / Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 2–6% net revenue gain by shifting 10% of OTA bookings to direct |
| Key Metrics | Channel contribution %, OTA commission rate, Direct booking %, CPA by channel |
| Blind Spot | Total revenue looks healthy, but net revenue after commissions (15–25% OTA fees) tells a different story; operators who don't track net-RevPAR by channel overpay for distribution |

### R03 — Demand Forecasting & Pace Analysis
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 3–8% RevPAR improvement through better anticipation of demand windows |
| Key Metrics | Pickup rate, Booking lead time, On-the-books (OTB) vs. budget, Segment mix (transient/group/contract) |
| Blind Spot | Without pace tracking, revenue managers react to occupancy instead of anticipating it; last-minute discounting destroys rate integrity |

### R04 — Group Displacement Analysis
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 1–4% total revenue recovery by rejecting unprofitable group blocks |
| Key Metrics | Group rate vs. BAR, Displaced transient revenue, Ancillary spend per group guest vs. transient |
| Blind Spot | Groups fill rooms but often at deep discounts that displace higher-yielding transient demand; without displacement modeling, sales teams accept any group that "fills the house" |

### R05 — Ancillary Revenue per Guest
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, F |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 8–20% of room revenue can come from ancillary (spa, F&B, parking, experiences) when actively managed |
| Key Metrics | TRevPAR (Total Revenue per Available Room), Ancillary revenue per occupied room, Upsell conversion rate, Guest LTV |
| Blind Spot | Most hotels track rooms revenue and F&B separately but never tie ancillary spend back to the guest profile; they can't identify which guest segments have the highest total wallet |

### R06 — Guest Lifetime Value (LTV) Segmentation
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 10–25% higher retention revenue when marketing spend is reallocated to high-LTV segments |
| Key Metrics | Repeat-stay frequency, Average spend per stay, Loyalty tier, Acquisition cost, Churn probability |
| Blind Spot | Equal marketing spend across all guests; top 20% of guests often generate 60%+ of revenue but receive the same communications as one-time visitors |

### R07 — Loyalty Program ROI
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | Loyalty members spend 20–40% more per stay; but program costs must be offset — typical net lift is 5–12% |
| Key Metrics | Member vs. non-member ADR, Redemption liability, Point breakage rate, Member direct-booking % |
| Blind Spot | Programs are launched as "me too" initiatives without tracking whether members actually spend more *net of program costs* |

### R08 — Menu Engineering Matrix (Kasavana-Smith)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | F |
| Impact Type | Rev+ / Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 3–7% F&B margin improvement through menu redesign |
| Key Metrics | Popularity index (% of total sales), Contribution margin per item, Classification (Star / Plow Horse / Puzzle / Dog) |
| Blind Spot | F&B managers know gross sales but not which items actually contribute to profit; a best-seller with 15% margin should be positioned differently than a slow mover at 70% margin |

### R09 — RevPASH Optimization (Revenue per Available Seat Hour)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | F |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 5–15% F&B revenue uplift through better table management and daypart optimization |
| Key Metrics | RevPASH, Average check, Covers per hour, Table turn time, Seat occupancy % |
| Blind Spot | Restaurants optimize for "full house" but not for revenue velocity; a 2-hour dinner at $50 underperforms two 50-minute lunches at $30 each from a seat-hour perspective |

### R10 — Upsell & Cross-sell Conversion Tracking
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, F, C |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 2–5% incremental revenue per transaction |
| Key Metrics | Upsell offer rate, Conversion rate, Incremental $ per upsell, Top-performing upsell items |
| Blind Spot | Upsells happen informally at check-in or in-app but are never tracked systematically; without data, there's no way to train staff or optimize the offer sequence |

### R11 — Revenue per Available Desk (RevPAD)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | C |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | Baseline metric — operators without RevPAD can't even tell if pricing is working |
| Key Metrics | RevPAD, Desk utilization %, Revenue per member per day, Mix (hot-desk vs. dedicated vs. private) |
| Blind Spot | Co-working operators focus on membership count, not revenue per unit of capacity; a "full" space can still be underpriced if the membership mix is wrong |

### R12 — Meeting Room Yield Management
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 10–30% meeting-space revenue uplift with time-based pricing |
| Key Metrics | RevPASM (Revenue per Available Square Meter per Hour), Utilization %, Cancellation/no-show rate, Ancillary (AV, catering) |
| Blind Spot | Meeting rooms are priced flat per half-day or full-day; peak hours (9–12 AM) subsidize off-peak, and no-shows waste 15–25% of capacity |

### R13 — Length-of-Stay Pricing
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Rev+ |
| Typical Impact | 2–5% RevPAR improvement by incentivizing longer stays that reduce turn costs |
| Key Metrics | ALOS, Revenue per guest-night by LOS bucket, Housekeeping cost per turnover |
| Blind Spot | Short stays have higher per-room turn costs; without LOS-based pricing, a 1-night guest at $200 is treated the same as a 4-night guest at $200/night despite very different profitability |

---

## Cost Reduction Analyses

### C01 — Labor Productivity & Scheduling Optimization
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | A |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 3–8% labor cost reduction (labor is typically 25–45% of total operating costs) |
| Key Metrics | Labor cost % of revenue, Rooms cleaned per hour, Covers per server per shift, Overtime hours %, Scheduled vs. actual hours |
| Blind Spot | Schedules are built on rules of thumb, not demand forecasts; overstaffing in low-demand periods and overtime in peaks both destroy margin |

### C02 — Energy Cost per Occupied Room (EPOR)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 10–25% energy cost reduction through occupancy-linked HVAC/lighting controls |
| Key Metrics | EPOR, kWh per room-night, Peak vs. off-peak consumption, Unoccupied-room energy draw |
| Blind Spot | Energy is treated as fixed overhead; in reality, 30–50% of building energy is consumed by unoccupied rooms/zones — linking HVAC to PMS occupancy data produces immediate savings |

### C03 — CPOR Benchmarking (Cost per Occupied Room)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 2–6% operating cost reduction when CPOR is tracked and benchmarked against USALI peers |
| Key Metrics | CPOR (total and by department), Amenity cost per room, Linen cost per room, Minibar cost vs. revenue |
| Blind Spot | Properties track total costs but not unit costs; a rising occupancy masks a rising CPOR — profitability per room can decline even as total revenue grows |

### C04 — Food & Beverage Cost Control
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | F |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 2–5% improvement in food cost % (industry target: 28–32% for full-service) |
| Key Metrics | Food cost %, Beverage cost %, Waste %, Portion variance, Supplier price tracking |
| Blind Spot | Kitchens know their food cost % at month-end but not in real-time; daily recipe-level tracking catches portion drift, waste, and supplier price creep before they erode a full month's margin |

### C05 — Procurement & Supplier Consolidation
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | A |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 3–10% savings on purchased goods through volume consolidation and competitive bidding |
| Key Metrics | Spend by category, Supplier count per category, Price variance vs. contract, Order frequency |
| Blind Spot | Multi-property operators often buy the same items from different suppliers at different prices; even single-property operators rarely audit whether contracted prices are being honored |

### C06 — Preventive Maintenance vs. Reactive Repair
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 15–30% reduction in maintenance costs; reactive repairs cost 3–5× more than preventive |
| Key Metrics | Work orders by type (preventive vs. reactive), Mean time between failures, Maintenance cost per room, Guest complaints related to maintenance |
| Blind Spot | Maintenance departments are measured on response time, not prevention rate; without tracking the ratio, the operation rewards firefighting instead of prevention |

### C07 — Overtime & Provisional Labor Leakage
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | A |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 1–3% of total labor cost is avoidable overtime; agency/temp labor premiums add 20–40% vs. in-house |
| Key Metrics | Overtime hours as % of total, Agency labor spend, Planned vs. unplanned absence rate, Cross-training coverage |
| Blind Spot | Overtime and agency costs are buried in department line items; they're only visible when broken out and tracked against scheduling accuracy |

### C08 — Utility Peak/Off-Peak Profiling
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 5–12% energy bill reduction by shifting deferrable loads to off-peak tariff windows |
| Key Metrics | kWh by time-of-day, Peak demand charges (kW), Tariff schedule, Laundry/kitchen run times |
| Blind Spot | Most operators pay demand charges (~30% of the bill) without knowing which equipment triggers peaks; shifting laundry or pre-cooling by 1–2 hours can dramatically reduce demand charges |

### C09 — Waste & Spoilage Tracking
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | F |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 2–5% of food purchases are wasted; active tracking reduces waste by 40–60% |
| Key Metrics | Waste by category (prep, plate, spoilage), Waste cost as % of food purchases, Par level accuracy |
| Blind Spot | Kitchens estimate waste; they don't weigh it. Without measurement, there's no accountability and no link between prep forecasting and actual demand |

### C10 — Water Consumption per Guest-Night
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Cost− |
| Typical Impact | 8–15% water cost reduction through linen-reuse programs, low-flow retrofits, and leak detection |
| Key Metrics | Liters per guest-night, Linen/towel reuse rate, Irrigation as % of total water, Leak incident frequency |
| Blind Spot | Water is a small line item that's never analyzed; but in resort/tropical properties, it can be 2–4% of total operating cost — material enough to manage |

---

## Dual-Impact Analyses (Revenue + Cost)

### D01 — Occupancy Break-Even & GOP Flow-Through
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | Decision framework — every 1% occupancy above break-even flows 60–80% to GOP |
| Key Metrics | Break-even occupancy %, Fixed vs. variable cost split, GOP flow-through rate, Marginal cost per room-night |
| Blind Spot | Operators know their occupancy but not their break-even point; below break-even, discounting accelerates losses; above it, incremental revenue is almost pure profit — without this analysis, pricing decisions are uninformed |

### D02 — Competitive Set Benchmarking (STR Indices)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | Properties with RGI < 100 have a defined revenue gap; closing RGI to parity = direct ADR/occupancy uplift |
| Key Metrics | RGI (Revenue Generation Index), MPI (Market Penetration Index), ARI (Average Rate Index) |
| Blind Spot | Internal metrics trend upward but the property is losing market share; without STR comp-set benchmarking, "growth" can mask competitive decline |

### D03 — Guest Satisfaction → Revenue Correlation
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, F |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | 1-point improvement in review score correlates with 1–5% ADR premium (Cornell research) |
| Key Metrics | OTA review score, NPS, CSAT, Complaint rate, Repeat-guest %, Review response rate |
| Blind Spot | Satisfaction is tracked as a quality metric, not a revenue metric; most operators don't model the dollar impact of a 0.5-point review improvement on pricing power |

### D04 — Tenant / Member Churn Prediction
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | C, H (extended-stay) |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | Reducing churn by 5 percentage points can increase annual revenue 15–25% (retained revenue + saved acquisition cost) |
| Key Metrics | Monthly churn rate, Churn by cohort/plan/tenure, Leading indicators (usage decline, support tickets, payment delays), Acquisition cost vs. LTV |
| Blind Spot | Operators measure churn as a lagging monthly statistic; by the time it's reported, the members have already left. Leading-indicator models enable proactive retention interventions |

### D05 — Space Utilization Heatmapping
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | C, H (conference/event) |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | 10–20% revenue uplift from underused zones (repurpose or reprice) + 5–10% cost reduction from closing underused zones during off-hours |
| Key Metrics | Utilization by zone/floor/hour, Revenue per sqm per hour, Foot traffic patterns |
| Blind Spot | Total utilization % hides cold zones; a building at "75% occupancy" may have one floor at 95% and another at 40% — without spatial granularity, there's no signal for repricing, repurposing, or consolidation |

### D06 — RevPAR Decomposition (Rate vs. Volume)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | Diagnostic — not a direct $ number but reveals whether RevPAR changes are rate-driven or occupancy-driven, which determines the correct corrective action |
| Key Metrics | RevPAR, ADR, Occupancy %, RevPAR index vs. prior year, Rate contribution vs. volume contribution |
| Blind Spot | RevPAR went up 5% — is that good? If ADR rose 8% but occupancy fell 3%, the hotel is pricing out demand. If occupancy rose 7% but ADR fell 2%, the hotel is discounting to fill. Different problems, different solutions |

### D07 — Segment Profitability Analysis
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | 2–6% margin improvement by reallocating capacity from low-margin to high-margin segments |
| Key Metrics | Revenue, CPOR, and GOP by segment (transient, corporate negotiated, group, wholesale, OTA), Net ADR after commissions |
| Blind Spot | Hotels report average ADR but not profitability by segment; a corporate contract at $180/night with no commission may be more profitable than an OTA booking at $220/night minus 22% commission |

### D08 — Daypart Revenue Analysis
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | F, C |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | 5–15% revenue increase by activating underperforming dayparts (e.g., afternoon café, late-night bar) |
| Key Metrics | Revenue by daypart, Covers/visits by hour, Average check by daypart, Labor cost by daypart |
| Blind Spot | F&B operations are optimized for lunch and dinner peaks; the 2–5 PM and post-9 PM windows are often dead but fully staffed. Targeted promotions or programming can activate these periods at minimal marginal cost |

### D09 — Cancellation & No-Show Pattern Analysis
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H, C |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | 1–3% occupancy recovery through smarter overbooking and tighter cancellation policies |
| Key Metrics | Cancellation rate by segment/channel/lead time, No-show rate, Revenue lost to unsold last-minute inventory |
| Blind Spot | Hotels apply a flat overbooking factor; in reality, cancellation rates vary dramatically by channel (OTA ~20–30% vs. direct ~5–10%) and by day-of-week — segment-specific overbooking models capture the difference |

### D10 — Total Revenue Management (TRevPAR)
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Segment | H |
| Impact Type | Both |
| Typical Impact | Framework shift — properties optimizing TRevPAR (rooms + F&B + spa + events + parking) outperform rooms-only RevPAR optimizers by 5–12% in total revenue |
| Key Metrics | TRevPAR, Revenue by profit center, Ancillary attach rate, Guest total spend |
| Blind Spot | Revenue management is siloed in rooms division; F&B, spa, and events have their own P&Ls. Nobody owns the guest's total wallet — and nobody optimizes across profit centers to maximize total property revenue |

---
